justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California wage transparency as legal requirement, Germany works council as blocking prerequisite, Indonesia as optional with no regulatory mandate. (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as invariant pre-condition in BR-4 with testable assertion. (3) Driver classification data model difference identified in BR-5 (employee vs contractor framing affects displayed fields). (4) Formal escalations raised for legal review across 4 distinct issues. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-7 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) Confidence level 45, well below the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on jurisdiction-specific rules — BR-1 through BR-8 each tied to specific legal statutes (CA Labor Code §226, BetrVG §87, AB5)
Works council approval modeled as a hard invariant (BR-4) with a testable gate, not just mentioned in passing
Driver classification difference captured as a display-model invariant (BR-5) with concrete field-level assertions (hours_worked/hourly_rate vs delivery_fee)
Surfaced the subtle trap about A/B testing being permitted in Indonesia but prohibited in California and requiring re-approval in Germany
Identified the EU Pay Transparency Directive (2026 enforcement) as a forward-looking regulatory risk for the German rollout — goes beyond what was expected
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform and explains jurisdiction-specific risk levels (HIGH CA, HIGH DE, LOW ID)
Confidence level of 45 with detailed reasoning about what is unknown — appropriately low given legal complexity
Edge cases are excellent — driver relocation, works council revocation, late tips, negative earnings, and A/B test permissibility all demonstrate deep domain thinking
Four formal escalations with options and recommendations, covering German classification, real-time definition, EU directive, and data retention

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California mandatory disclosure (BR-3, BR-4), Germany works council gate (BR-5), Indonesia voluntary (BR-7). (2) Germany Betriebsrat approval explicitly identified as a hard pre-condition/blocking gate in BR-5 and escalation. (3) Driver classification data model difference identified — employee (CA) vs contractor (Indonesia) in ubiquitous language and BR-3/BR-7. (4) Formal escalation for legal review present with structured options and recommendations. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-7 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis with correct legal citations (AB5, CA Labor Code §351/§224, BetrVG §87(1)(6))
Betriebsrat approval modeled as a hard deployment gate with feature flag check (BR-5, AC-2), not just a mention
Driver classification difference clearly surfaced in ubiquitous language and reflected throughout business rules
Four well-structured escalations covering ambiguity and missing context, each with options and recommendations
Edge case about conditional Betriebsrat approval captures the subtle trap of works council negotiations altering feature design
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout requirement and recommends excluding Germany from v1
Confidence at 52 with clearly articulated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions — appropriately humble given legal complexity
Captures the subtle trap that California transparency is legally mandatory and cannot be removed to simplify the global codebase (implicit in BR-3's invariant:true designation)

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California wage transparency as legal requirement, Germany works council as blocking pre-condition, Indonesia as optional. (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard deployment gate in BR-4 with BetrVG §87 citation. (3) Driver classification data model difference identified in BR-6 (employee under AB5 in CA vs. independent contractor in Indonesia). (4) Formal escalation for legal review in escalations section with explicit recommendation to mandate legal review as a hard gate before any jurisdiction launches. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-4 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis with specific legal citations (Labor Code §226, §351, AB5, BetrVG §87(1) No. 6)
Works council approval modeled as a hard deployment gate with feature flag check, not just a mention — includes revocation edge case
Driver classification correctly identified as a legal fact determined by jurisdiction (BR-6), not a configurable attribute
Surfaces the subtle trap about removing transparency features: edge case about deductions pushing below minimum wage in CA, and the new-jurisdiction fail-safe defaulting to most restrictive rules
Confidence at 52 with well-articulated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout requirement and that Germany introduces unpredictable timeline dependency
Edge cases are excellent — cross-jurisdiction drivers, works council revocation mid-rollout, negative tips, pay period rollover, zero-delivery shifts
Escalations are formal and structured with options and recommendations, not informal suggestions
Identifies that Indonesia's lack of legal requirement makes transparency a business/competitive decision (BR-7 marked invariant: false)
